title: Red Queen Effect

content.statement: In a co-evolving market, you must run faster just to stay in place.
content.question: Am I improving at the speed of my market, or quietly falling behind?
content.equation: Competitive Position = Internal Improvement Rate ÷ Market Improvement Rate
content.mental_model: Treadmill, keep running, or fall behind.
content.business_lens: Bake continuous improvement into operations; allocate R&D in mature businesses despite the temptation to harvest.
content.life_lens: In any competitive arena, comfort with current pace is decline.
sop: Map your improvement rate vs market average
Identify where you are below the curve
Add disruptive bets to outpace incumbents elsewhere
content.explainer: Nokia and Blockbuster both stopped running at market pace; Apple and Netflix kept accelerating. The Red Queen Effect is why mature industries are still demanding, the floor moves under your feet whether you do or not.
questions: Am I improving at the speed of my market, or quietly falling behind?
What did your top competitor ship or improve in the last 90 days that you haven't matched or countered yet?
If you froze your product exactly as it is today, how many months before customers would notice you'd stopped moving?
content.equation_example: Internal Improvement Rate: 3 meaningful improvements shipped per quarter. Market Improvement Rate: 4 meaningful improvements per quarter across competitors. 3 divided by 4 equals 75% of market pace. Above 100% means you're pulling ahead of the field; below 100% means the floor is moving faster than you are, even if you feel busy.
content.closing_statement: Standing still is falling behind in a moving market.

caption:
Principle Nº 319: Red Queen Effect

In a co-evolving market, you must run faster just to stay in place. Nokia and Blockbuster both stopped running at market pace; Apple and Netflix kept accelerating.

Ask yourself: Am I improving at the speed of my market, or quietly falling behind?
